text: Which of the following correctly describes the functional difference between a 'gas-powered' and an 'electrically-powered' transport ventilator, and the clinical implications for retrieval?

options.A: Gas-powered ventilators are always safer and should be preferred in all retrieval situations
options.B: Gas-powered ventilators are not affected by altitude, unlike electrically-powered ventilators
options.C: Gas-powered ventilators use compressed gas (oxygen or air) as both the driving force and the gas delivered to the patient
options.D: Electrically-powered ventilators are not aviation-certified and cannot be used in helicopters
options.E: The distinction between gas-powered and electrically-powered ventilators has no clinical relevance during retrieval

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.3.1(b) covers ventilatory support devices, and the ABC of Transfer (Chapter 11) provides a clear classification. This distinction has significant practical implications for oxygen calculation and mission planning: Gas-powered ventilators (e.g. Oxylog 1000, Pneupac series): oxygen serves both as the driving mechanism and the inspired gas; total oxygen consumption = FiO₂ × Minute Volume + driving gas (often equal to or greater than minute volume); results in higher total oxygen consumption, particularly at higher FiO₂; advantage: function without electrical power. Electrically-powered ventilators (e.g. Hamilton T1, Oxylog 3000+): an electric motor/turbine drives the bellows or controls gas flow; oxygen consumption ≈ FiO₂ × Minute Volume only (no driving gas requirement); significantly lower oxygen consumption for the same ventilation parameters; advantage: oxygen-efficient for long missions; disadvantage: requires battery or mains power supply. Clinical relevance: for long-duration retrievals, oxygen-limited platforms (remote missions, international repatriation), or high FiO₂ requirements, selecting an electrically-powered ventilator may be essential to ensure adequate oxygen supply for the mission duration. Option E incorrectly dismisses the clinical relevance. Option B is incorrect — both types are affected by altitude via Boyle's Law in different ways.

text: A retrieval team performing a primary response to a remote rural location loses mobile phone signal. The vehicle's radio system is a TETRA (Terrestrial Trunked Radio) handset. Which of the following correctly describes the advantage of TETRA over standard analogue radio and mobile phones in this context?

options.A: TETRA is only available to police services — ambulance and retrieval services use separate systems
options.B: TETRA is a digital, encrypted, secure network used by UK and Irish emergency services
options.C: TETRA replaces the need for all other communication devices — no backup is required
options.D: TETRA handsets have a battery life of more than 48 hours without recharging
options.E: TETRA is superior because it operates on the same frequency as commercial FM radio, giving broader coverage

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.4.1(a) covers communications equipment. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (Chapter 18) specifically describes TETRA: "Terrestrial trunked radio (TETRA) is the digital, secure network utilised by all UK emergency services. Unlike conventional analogue radio, it boasts complete coverage utilising multiple networks and allows for interoperability between services. It has the ability to be used as a radio, broadcasting to a group channel, individual to individual or as a mobile phone. It is secure, will source signal even when in enclosed spaces and has the benefit of being flight tested and used by HEMS units." Key advantages: (1) Interoperability — police, fire, ambulance, and other agencies can communicate on the same system; (2) Security — encrypted, preventing eavesdropping (important for patient confidentiality); (3) Coverage — multiple network towers provide coverage in areas of poor mobile phone signal; (4) Functionality — radio and telephone capability in one device. Option D overstates battery life — TETRA handsets typically have 8–12 hours battery life, and "spare batteries are carried on many, if not all, emergency service vehicles" per the ABC. Option C is incorrect — the ABC notes that "mobile phones add an extra level of resilience" — redundancy in communication is important.

text: Regarding the use of basic navigation equipment during ground retrieval, which of the following most accurately describes the role of the medical crew member in navigation?

options.A: The medical crew should take over driving when the driver needs to focus on navigation in unfamiliar areas
options.B: The medical crew member may appropriately assist with navigation using map reading, GPS devices, or smartphone applications
options.C: Navigation is irrelevant for retrieval teams who always respond to known hospital locations
options.D: Navigation is exclusively the driver's responsibility — the medical crew should focus entirely on the patient
options.E: GPS navigation systems eliminate all need for map reading skills in retrieval — paper maps are obsolete

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.4.1(c) covers basic navigation equipment including map reading, and subdomain 3.4.6 covers the co-driver role. Navigation in the retrieval context is a genuine crew resource management challenge — the medical crew member may be the only person available to assist the driver with route-finding in remote or unfamiliar environments. Key principles: (1) Safety hierarchy — patient monitoring is the primary clinical duty and must not be compromised for navigation assistance; (2) Task allocation — in a two-person medical crew, one may assist with navigation while the other maintains sole patient focus; a single medical crew member must prioritise clinical monitoring over navigation assistance; (3) Equipment knowledge — GPS devices, mapping applications (e.g. Google Maps, What3words for remote locations), and traditional map reading are all relevant skills for retrieval teams operating in rural and remote settings; (4) Pre-mission preparation — obtaining route information and downloading maps to GPS/phone before departure reduces in-flight navigation demands; (5) Communication — the medical crew can communicate turn-by-turn directions to the driver clearly and concisely without creating distraction. Option E is incorrect and complacent — GPS can fail (battery, signal loss, mapping errors) and paper map reading remains a genuine retrieval competency. Option A is explicitly prohibited by driving law and professional standards.

text: Under the Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001 (UK) and equivalent Irish legislation (Misuse of Drugs Acts 1977 and 1984), which of the following represents the correct approach to carrying, storing, and accounting for controlled drugs in the retrieval setting?

options.A: Controlled drug regulations do not apply during emergency clinical situations — any available clinician can administer any controlled drug without documentation
options.B: The rules for controlled drugs only apply to opioids; other schedule 3 drugs (e.g. ketamine) can be managed without special documentation
options.C: Controlled drugs must be stored in a dedicated, locked, secure container; a precise, contemporaneous record must be kept of all controlled drugs drawn up, administered, wasted, or returned
options.D: Controlled drugs can be carried in any locked container as long as the key is held by the most senior clinician on the mission
options.E: Controlled drug records only need to be completed at the end of the shift, not contemporaneously

explanation: DipRTM subdomains 4.5.5 and 4.5.13 directly address controlled drug legislation and compliance in retrieval. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (Chapter 19) and the Oxford Specialist Handbook both emphasise that controlled drug regulations apply in the retrieval setting with the same rigour as in hospital. Key requirements under UK and Irish law: (1) Secure storage — controlled drugs must be stored in a locked, dedicated container that meets regulatory requirements; (2) Contemporaneous records — entries must be made at the time of drug handling, not retrospectively; (3) Two-person verification — a second registered practitioner must witness and co-sign all controlled drug transactions; (4) Complete documentation — every transaction (draw-up, administration, waste, return) must be documented with patient ID, drug name and strength, batch number, amount drawn, amount given, amount wasted, and dual signatures; (5) Waste disposal — wasted controlled drugs must be rendered irretrievable (e.g. mixed with absorbent material) and witnessed; (6) Storage location and quantity must reconcile with records at all times. Failures in controlled drug documentation during retrieval represent both a regulatory violation and a medico-legal risk. Option A is incorrect — emergency clinical situations do not suspend legal requirements, though practical adaptations may be necessary. Option B is incorrect — ketamine is a Schedule 3 controlled drug with specific handling requirements in the UK (POM classification in Ireland).

text: When preparing for a 3-hour retrieval of a ventilated patient on FiO₂ 0.6 and minute ventilation of 8 L/min using a gas-powered transport ventilator, which of the following most accurately estimates the oxygen requirement and appropriate cylinder selection?

options.A: Total oxygen needed = FiO₂ × MV + ventilator driving gas consumption. For a gas-powered ventilator: total flow ≈ (0.6 ×
options.B: Oxygen calculation is unnecessary — modern ventilators regulate their own supply automatically
options.C: The patient's FiO₂ does not affect oxygen consumption calculations
options.D: 3 hours × 8 L/min = 1,440 L; one size-F cylinder (680 L) will be adequate
options.E: One size-F cylinder provides 6 hours of oxygen for any ventilated patient

explanation: Oxygen calculation is a core pre-mission competency examined in DipRTM (subdomain 4.5.6 — listing medical gases, and 1.4.4 — items required for retrieval). The ABC of Transfer states: "One of the fundamental principles of good transfer practice is preparation and consequently ensuring the provision of sufficient oxygen, particularly in ventilated patients, is paramount." For a gas-powered ventilator: total oxygen consumption = FiO₂ × minute volume (gas delivered to patient) + ventilator driving gas (typically approximately equal to MV for simple pneumatic ventilators). Calculation: Patient gas: 0.6 × 8 = 4.8 L/min; Ventilator driving gas: ≈8 L/min; Total: ≈12.8 L/min. For 3 hours (180 min): 12.8 × 180 = 2,304 L. Safety reserve (FICM recommends carrying ≥1.5× requirement): 2,304 × 1.5 = 3,456 L. Size-F cylinder = 680 L; three cylinders = 2,040 L (below requirement); four cylinders = 2,720 L (adequate with some reserve). Therefore, four size-F cylinders minimum. This calculation is also tested in context of: oxygen-limited platforms (helicopter, remote missions), long-duration retrievals, and patients requiring high FiO₂ (FiO₂ 1.0 × 8 L/min = 8 L/min patient gas alone). Option D fails to account for driving gas and provides insufficient oxygen for the mission. Option C is incorrect — FiO₂ is a direct multiplier in patient gas consumption.

text: A retrieval physician is drawing up a ketamine infusion for an agitated patient. The stock ampoule reads: Ketamine 500 mg/10 ml (50 mg/ml). The physician requires a 1 mg/kg/hr infusion for a 70 kg patient to be delivered via syringe driver. The infusion will be made up to 50 ml total. What concentration of ketamine (mg/ml) is in the prepared syringe, and at what rate (ml/hr) should the syringe driver be set?

options.A: Concentration 5 mg/ml; rate 14 ml/hr
options.B: Concentration 10 mg/ml; rate 3.5 ml/hr
options.C: Concentration 1 mg/ml; rate 70 ml/hr
options.D: Concentration 10 mg/ml; rate 7 ml/hr
options.E: Concentration 2 mg/ml; rate 35 ml/hr

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.10–4.5.12 covers safe preparation and administration of medicines, including parenteral infusions. This calculation skill is patient safety critical in retrieval. Step 1 — Determine dose required: 1 mg/kg/hr × 70 kg = 70 mg/hr. Step 2 — Prepare syringe: Draw up ketamine from the 50 mg/ml stock ampoule. To make a 50 ml syringe: take 10 ml of ketamine (500 mg) and dilute to 50 ml total with 0.9% NaCl = 500 mg in 50 ml = 10 mg/ml. Step 3 — Calculate rate: Rate (ml/hr) = Dose required (mg/hr) ÷ Concentration (mg/ml) = 70 ÷ 10 = 7 ml/hr. Verification: 7 ml/hr × 10 mg/ml = 70 mg/hr = 1 mg/kg/hr for 70 kg. ✓ Key safety checks: (1) Confirm concentration before setting the rate; (2) Label the syringe clearly with drug, concentration, patient, date, and time; (3) Double-check the calculation with a second team member (when available); (4) Set the syringe driver rate correctly. Option A represents 250 mg in 50 ml (5 mg/ml) — this would result from using only 5 ml of the stock ampoule; the rate of 14 ml/hr would also deliver 70 mg/hr, so the dose is correct but the preparation differs from the most concentrated practical option. Both A and B deliver the correct dose; however, option D uses the full ampoule and is the standard preparation described.

text: Under Civil Aviation Authority and Irish Aviation Authority regulations, which of the following correctly describes the requirements for carrying medical oxygen cylinders in an aircraft?

options.A: There are specific aviation regulations governing the carriage of oxygen in aircraft: cylinders must be certified for aviation use, properly
options.B: Only liquid oxygen is permitted in aircraft — compressed gas cylinders are prohibited
options.C: Oxygen cylinders are classified as non-hazardous for aviation purposes and require no special declaration
options.D: Medical oxygen cylinders can be carried in any quantity without restriction, as oxygen is a standard medical gas
options.E: Medical oxygen cylinders must be carried in the cargo hold and cannot be in the patient cabin

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.8 requires understanding of aviation regulations for medical gases. Oxygen is classified as a hazardous material in aviation (UN1072 — Oxygen, compressed) due to its: (1) Oxidising properties — greatly accelerates combustion and fire; (2) High pressure — failure of a pressurised cylinder in an aircraft can cause structural damage; (3) Displacement of cabin air if leaking. Aviation requirements: (1) Aviation-certified cylinders — must meet aviation engineering standards (typically BS/ISO certified for aerospace use); (2) Securing — cylinders must be restrained in approved holders to prevent movement during turbulence or manoeuvring; (3) Fire hazard — must not be near heat sources, electrical equipment, or flammable materials; (4) Declaration — must be declared to the aircraft captain as hazardous cargo; (5) Documentation — IATA dangerous goods regulations may apply for commercial aircraft; (6) Quantity — sufficient for patient needs plus safety reserve, but not excessive quantities that increase fire risk. The ABC of Transfer notes: "equipment in vehicles should be flight-tested to ensure compatibility with the aircraft's avionics systems, secured." The Oxford Specialist Handbook's discussion of aviation physiology includes oxygen management as a critical safety consideration. Failure to properly secure or declare oxygen cylinders has contributed to aircraft incidents internationally.

text: A trauma patient being retrieved requires transfusion of O-negative uncrossmatched packed red blood cells (PRBCs) during transfer. Which of the following represents the most important consideration when using blood products in the retrieval environment?

options.A: Only group-specific crossmatched blood can be used during retrieval — uncrossmatched blood is not permitted outside of the hospital setting
options.B: Blood products should be warmed to body temperature in the ambulance heater before administration
options.C: Temperature control is critical
options.D: Blood products are stable at any temperature for up to 24 hours, so temperature control is not necessary in retrieval
options.E: O-negative blood can be administered at any temperature — the patient's core temperature will restore the blood to normal before it reaches the heart

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.9 addresses blood products and the operational challenges of their use. The ABC of Transfer (Chapter 15) provides specific guidance on blood product carriage in retrieval (Table 15.7). Key requirements: (1) Temperature control — PRBCs must be maintained at 2–6°C; temperatures outside this range cause haemolysis, bacterial proliferation, and impaired oxygen delivery; specialised portable blood transport containers (e.g. Minnesota Thermal Science box with data loggers) are required; (2) Traceability — every unit must be linked to the patient receiving it; blood group, unit number, expiry date, and patient ID must be documented; (3) Monitoring — data loggers provide continuous temperature recording that is downloadable to demonstrate regulatory compliance; (4) Governance — SOPs, team training, and ongoing quality assurance are required for any service carrying blood products; (5) Warming — blood warming before IV administration (using approved in-line blood warmers) prevents hypothermia but this is a clinical decision, not done in an ambulance heater (option B is dangerous — ambulance heaters are uncontrolled and can cause haemolysis). O-negative blood (option A) is the emergency universal donor and its use without crossmatching is supported by major trauma guidelines (NICE NG39, ATLS) when time does not permit formal crossmatching. Cold blood at scale (multiple units) does cause clinically significant hypothermia (option E is incorrect).

text: A retrieval team discovers on arrival at a referring hospital that a critical infusion of vasopressin for a patient with septic shock was running through the same IV line as a blood product transfusion that was completed 20 minutes ago. The blood product line was flushed with 0.9% saline before the vasopressin was connected. The patient's BP is now 80/45 mmHg. What is the most appropriate immediate action and which medication compatibility principle does this situation illustrate?

options.A: Blood products should never be co-administered with vasopressors through the same line — however, if the blood product line was thoroughly flushed with saline, vasopressin can safely run through the same line; focus should be on restoring BP
options.B: All medications are compatible with blood products once the line is flushed — no action is needed
options.C: Establish new dedicated IV access for vasopressors; the blood product line should be used only for fluids and blood products; treat the hypotension urgently
options.D: The low blood pressure is unrelated to the IV line issue — check the vasopressin infusion rate and increase it
options.E: The vasopressin was mixed with blood — discard the syringe, establish new IV access, and restart the infusion through a clean dedicated line

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.2 (safe prescribing in retrieval) and 4.5.16 (safe administration of blood products) together underpin this question. This scenario illustrates drug compatibility principles in the retrieval and critical care setting. Vasopressors (noradrenaline, vasopressin, phenylephrine) should ideally be administered through dedicated central venous access to: (1) Ensure reliable delivery without interruption; (2) Prevent incompatibility with other infusions; (3) Allow rate titration without affecting co-administered drugs; (4) Reduce risk of extravasation injury (vasopressors can cause severe tissue necrosis if extravasated through peripheral access). Blood products have specific compatibility restrictions: only 0.9% sodium chloride or ABO-compatible blood products should co-infuse through blood administration sets — hypotonic solutions (5% dextrose) cause haemolysis; calcium-containing solutions (Hartmann's, Ringer's Lactate) cause clotting of citrate-anticoagulated blood. In this scenario: the immediate priority is treating the hypotension. While the vasopressin infusion compatibility question is secondary, best practice is to establish dedicated vasopressor access separate from the blood/fluid line. This reflects the FICM, BCSH (British Committee for Standards in Haematology), and SHOT guidelines on blood product administration safety, which are directly applicable to the retrieval context.

text: A retrieval physician needs to administer intravenous ketamine for RSI. The stock is: Ketamine 200 mg/2 ml (100 mg/ml). The required induction dose is 1.5 mg/kg IV for a 80 kg patient. What volume of undiluted ketamine should be administered?

options.A: 3.2 ml
options.B: 1.6 ml
options.C: 0.8 ml
options.D: 2.4 ml
options.E: 1.2 ml

explanation: Safe drug calculation is tested directly in DipRTM (subdomain 4.5.10–4.5.12). Step 1 — Calculate dose: 1.5 mg/kg × 80 kg = 120 mg. Step 2 — Calculate volume: Volume = Dose ÷ Concentration = 120 mg ÷ 100 mg/ml = 1.2 ml. Verification: 1.2 ml × 100 mg/ml = 120 mg = 1.5 mg/kg for 80 kg ✓. Clinical notes: IV ketamine at 1.5 mg/kg produces dissociative anaesthesia appropriate for RSI; for IM use the dose is higher (4–6 mg/kg). The concentration of 100 mg/ml is standard for ketamine ampoules — this high concentration means small volumes for IV use; care must be taken not to confuse the IM concentration (typically used undiluted) with the IV route (where dilution to 10 mg/ml is recommended for IV boluses to prevent rapid injection); however for RSI, the undiluted form diluted with a saline flush is acceptable given the time-critical nature. The question specifically asks for undiluted volume — 1.2 ml. Option C (0.8 ml) = 80 mg = 1 mg/kg (underdose). Option D (2.4 ml) = 240 mg = 3 mg/kg (overdose, more appropriate for IM use). Option B (1.6 ml) = 160 mg = 2 mg/kg (excessive IV induction dose). Option A (3.2 ml) = 320 mg = 4 mg/kg (IM RSI dose — inappropriate IV). Drug calculation errors in retrieval are a significant cause of patient harm and are a focus of the DipRTM patient safety curriculum.

text: When transporting a patient requiring a rocuronium infusion for continued neuromuscular blockade during a 2-hour transfer, which of the following correctly identifies the specific reversal agent that must be available in the retrieval kit, and the clinical scenario in which it would be required?

options.A: Sugammadex is only used for surgical patients in theatre — it has no role in the retrieval or prehospital setting
options.B: There is no reversal agent for rocuronium — once given, neuromuscular blockade must wear off spontaneously
options.C: Sugammadex (a modified gamma-cyclodextrin) is the specific reversal agent for rocuronium and vecuronium; it achieves rapid reversal even from deep neuromuscular blockade
options.D: Neostigmine and glycopyrrolate are the standard reversal agents for rocuronium; they should be used whenever the patient shows signs of spontaneous recovery
options.E: Flumazenil is the reversal agent for rocuronium in the retrieval setting

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.3 (categorising medicines used in retrieval) and clinical domain 1.4 both require understanding of reversal agents. Sugammadex (trade name Bridion) is a selective relaxant binding agent specifically developed to reverse aminosteroid neuromuscular blocking drugs (rocuronium, vecuronium). It encapsulates the drug in a stable complex, achieving rapid reversal. Critical retrieval relevance: Rocuronium is the preferred paralytic agent for RSI in many retrieval services (replacing suxamethonium in many protocols, particularly in the UK/Ireland), offering: predictable dosing, no contraindications in hyperkalaemia, and reversibility. If rocuronium is used for RSI in a difficult airway scenario and the patient cannot be intubated (cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate — CICO), sugammadex 16 mg/kg IV achieves full reversal of 1.2 mg/kg rocuronium in approximately 3 minutes, potentially allowing return of spontaneous ventilation. This is explicitly recognised in the DAS Difficult Airway Guidelines 2015 and AAGBI guidance. Every kit carrying rocuronium for RSI must also carry a full reversal dose of sugammadex. Neostigmine (option D) does not reverse deep rocuronium block adequately and has significant cardiac side effects. Option B is incorrect — sugammadex provides complete reversal. Option A is incorrect — sugammadex is used by UK and Irish retrieval and prehospital services routinely.

text: Which of the following routes of drug administration is most appropriate when IV access has failed and rapid absorption is required in a haemodynamically compromised patient during retrieval?

options.A: Subcutaneous injection — provides rapid and predictable absorption
options.B: Intramuscular injection — provides faster onset than IO in haemodynamic compromise
options.C: Oral administration — reliable and widely available
options.D: Sublingual administration — provides the fastest onset of all non-IV routes for all drugs
options.E: Intraosseous (IO) route

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.12 and 4.3.1(d) cover routes of drug administration and vascular access devices. The ABC of Transfer (Chapter 19: Routes of Administration) comprehensively covers this. In haemodynamic compromise, subcutaneous and intramuscular routes (option C, B, D) are unreliable because peripheral vasoconstriction reduces perfusion of peripheral tissues — drugs injected into subcutaneous fat or muscle may not be absorbed for hours, or may be rapidly washed into the central circulation as vasoconstriction releases (potentially causing delayed drug toxicity). IO access through the proximal tibia (adults: tibial tuberosity, adults can also use humeral head) using an EZ-IO drill or manual IO needle provides: (1) Reliable access to the medullary cavity of the bone, which communicates directly with the central venous circulation via the nutrient artery; (2) Drug absorption comparable in onset and peak concentration to IV delivery; (3) Suitability for all resuscitation drugs, crystalloids, blood products, and vasopressors; (4) Maintenance flow rates up to 80 ml/min with pressure infusion; (5) Rapid establishment (typically <60 seconds with EZ-IO). JRCALC guidelines recommend IO access as the first rescue vascular access route when IV fails in both cardiac arrest and critically ill patients. Sublingual (option D) provides rapid absorption for nitroglycerin and some other specific drugs but not all resuscitation agents.

text: A retrieval team is managing a patient requiring rapid sequence induction. After administration of 100 mg suxamethonium, the first intubation attempt with a Macintosh blade reveals a grade 3 view. A bougie-assisted attempt also fails. SpO₂ is 90% and falling. The DAS failed intubation algorithm is being followed. Which device should be immediately attempted as the next step in the 'Plan B' airway phase?

options.A: Perform an immediate surgical cricothyroidotomy
options.B: Allow the patient to wake up by reversing the suxamethonium
options.C: Attempt direct laryngoscopy a third time with maximum external laryngeal manipulation
options.D: Insert a second-generation supraglottic airway device (e.g. i-gel or LMA Supreme) as a rescue oxygenation device
options.E: Attempt video laryngoscopy without delay

explanation: DipRTM subdomains 1.4 (preparation for retrieval including airway planning) and 4.3.1(a) (airway management devices) both require knowledge of the DAS 2015 Difficult Airway guidelines. The DAS algorithm for unanticipated difficult intubation during RSI: Plan A — direct laryngoscopy (maximum 3 attempts including 1 with alternative device); if Plan A fails → declare "failed intubation"; Plan B — supraglottic airway device (second-generation LMA: i-gel, LMA Supreme, ProSeal) for rescue oxygenation; if Plan B fails → Plan C — face mask ventilation; if all facemask/supraglottic options fail → Plan D — emergency surgical airway (front of neck access — scalpel cricothyroidotomy). The 'cannot intubate, can oxygenate' scenario (Plan B success) allows time to waken the patient (if suxamethonium was used) or optimise conditions. With suxamethonium (option B): unlike rocuronium, suxamethonium cannot be pharmacologically reversed; however, its short duration (3–5 minutes) means spontaneous recovery of neuromuscular function occurs, allowing return of spontaneous breathing if SpO₂ can be maintained through supraglottic oxygenation. Video laryngoscopy (option E) is a valid Plan A alternative but should have been deployed during Plan A, not as a Plan B after two failed attempts without first securing oxygenation. Surgical airway (option A) is Plan D — not yet indicated if supraglottic oxygenation has not been attempted.

text: When transferring a patient who is currently receiving total parenteral nutrition (TPN) via a central venous catheter, what is the most appropriate approach to the TPN during transfer?

options.A: TPN must be completed before transfer can commence
options.B: TPN infusion rate should be doubled to compensate for any missed doses during the transfer period
options.C: Continue TPN at the current rate throughout transfer — interruption to nutritional support causes significant harm
options.D: TPN should generally be discontinued before transfer
options.E: TPN can be administered via peripheral IV access during transfer if the CVC port is needed for vasopressors

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5 and the ABC of Transfer (Chapter 5: Resuscitation and Stabilisation) both address this practical management issue. The ABC states: "Any therapies and infusions not required for immediate management during primary, or delayed primary, transfers (e.g. nutrition, thromboprophylaxis, stress ulcer prophylaxis) should be discontinued to minimise clinical and logistic complications." TPN represents a non-emergency infusion that: (1) Occupies a dedicated lumen of the central venous catheter that may be needed for vasopressors or other critical infusions; (2) Is complex to manage (specific tubing, filter requirements, compatibility issues); (3) Requires refrigeration for stability (impractical in the retrieval setting); (4) Has a risk of infection if the giving set is disconnected and reconnected; (5) Has no immediate clinical impact if discontinued for 2–6 hours in a well-nourished patient. Option E is incorrect — TPN must not be administered via peripheral IV access due to the high osmolarity causing severe venous thrombosis and tissue necrosis (peripheral PN requires specifically formulated low-osmolarity preparations). Option B (doubling rate) is dangerous — rapid infusion of TPN can cause hyperglycaemia, electrolyte disturbances, and metabolic complications.

text: Under Irish regulations, a retrieval physician has administered morphine 5 mg IV to a patient during transfer. Upon arriving at the receiving hospital, 3 mg morphine remains in the drawn-up syringe. What is the most appropriate management of the unused controlled drug?

options.A: Retain the remaining morphine in the syringe and return it to the retrieval vehicle drug supply for future use
options.B: Controlled drug wastage documentation is only required for Schedule 2 drugs — morphine does not require documentation
options.C: Waste the morphine by rendering it irretrievable, witnessed and co-signed by a second registered practitioner, with contemporaneous documentation in the controlled drug registeramount wasted, time, and both witnesses' signatures
options.D: Administer the remaining 3 mg to the patient on arrival to avoid wastage
options.E: Leave the remaining syringe at the receiving hospital for the ward team to administer at their discretion

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.13 (compliance with controlled drug legislation) requires knowledge of controlled drug disposal requirements. In Ireland, morphine is a Schedule 2 controlled drug under the Misuse of Drugs Acts 1977 and 1984 (equivalent to Schedule 2 under the UK Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2001). Disposal of unused controlled drugs: (1) Destruction — the drug must be rendered irretrievable; this means mixing with an absorbent material (specific denaturing kits), flushing, or returning to the pharmacy (depending on service policy); (2) Witnessed destruction — a second registered healthcare professional must witness and co-sign the disposal; (3) Documentation — the controlled drug register must be updated contemporaneously with: drug name, dose drawn up, dose administered, dose wasted, time of disposal, and signatures of both the administering clinician and the witness; (4) The amounts must balance — dose drawn = dose administered + dose wasted. Option A (returning drawn-up drugs to the supply) is prohibited — controlled drugs that have been prepared in a patient-care setting cannot be returned to stock. Option E (leaving at the ward) is a transfer of controlled drugs without appropriate documentation and chain of custody — this is illegal. Option B is incorrect — morphine is specifically a Schedule 2 drug requiring the strictest documentation.

text: A retrieval team has successfully intubated a patient using video laryngoscopy with a C-MAC device. Following confirmation of tube placement with capnography, the C-MAC device fails to power on when needed to confirm ETT position during a later patient deterioration. What principle of equipment governance does this scenario illustrate, and what is the correct immediate response?

options.A: The patient should be extubated and reintubated using a different technique
options.B: The team should attempt to repair the C-MAC during the retrieval
options.C: Equipment failure during retrieval requires an immediate return to the referring hospital
options.D: Video laryngoscopes are optional equipment; the team can manage without it
options.E: This illustrates the need for equipment redundancy and battery maintenance

explanation: DipRTM subdomains 4.1 (equipment governance) and 4.3.5–4.3.6 (operating devices and managing failures) are both tested here. This scenario illustrates two governance principles: (1) Battery management — video laryngoscope batteries must be checked and fully charged before each mission; underestimating mission duration is a common cause of battery failure (ABC: "Underestimation of duration of transport is a frequent cause of equipment battery failure"); (2) Equipment redundancy — retrieval services must carry backup equipment for all critical functions; for airway management, this means carrying both a video laryngoscope AND a direct laryngoscopy kit (standard Macintosh blades) — one cannot substitute for the other in all scenarios but direct laryngoscopy is the essential fallback. Immediate clinical response: assess the patient clinically (consciousness, SpO₂, ETCO₂ waveform, bilateral chest movement); these confirm ETT position without the video laryngoscope; if clinical repositioning of the tube is required, use direct laryngoscopy with the available Macintosh blade. Governance response: document the failure, report through the incident reporting system (where equipment failures are tracked to prevent recurrence), remove the device from service, and ensure replacement before the next mission. This reflects the Oxford Specialist Handbook principle that "all equipment must be maintained to the highest level of biomedical support and be fitted with appropriate auditory and visual alert systems."

text: Which of the following best describes the principle of equipment governance in the retrieval setting?

options.A: Equipment governance means ensuring all equipment is new and purchased from a single approved manufacturer
options.B: Equipment governance is the exclusive responsibility of the biomedical engineering department — clinical staff have no role
options.C: Equipment governance applies only to aircraft-mounted medical equipment, not to items carried in the retrieval kit bag
options.D: Standardisation of equipment is unnecessary as experienced clinicians can adapt to any device
options.E: Equipment governance covers selection, procurement, standardisation, maintenance, pre-mission checking, in-mission management, and post-mission servicing throughout the lifecycle

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.1.2 defines the principles of equipment governance. The Oxford Specialist Handbook emphasises: "Equipment must meet the needs of the patient population or therapeutic interventions, and must consider the operating environment, mission duration, availability of electrical power in transport platforms, oxygen consumption, and standard oxygen supplies available in vehicles." Equipment governance in retrieval encompasses: (1) Selection — choosing equipment appropriate for the retrieval environment (lightweight, robust, battery-capable, aviation-certified); (2) Standardisation — all team members can use all equipment confidently, including at night and in emergencies; (3) Maintenance — biomedical engineering schedules, calibration, and software updates; (4) Pre-mission checking — equipment checks using standardised checklists before every deployment; (5) In-mission management — monitoring battery life, oxygen levels, consumable stocks; (6) Post-mission servicing — decontamination, restock, faults reported. Clinical staff have an active role in all phases — failure to check equipment before departure is a clinical governance failure that has led to adverse patient events. Option D is dangerous — standardisation specifically prevents the cognitive burden of operating unfamiliar equipment under stress.

text: All medical equipment used in aeromedical retrieval must meet specific regulatory standards before it can be installed or used in an aircraft. Which of the following correctly describes aviation certification for medical equipment?

options.A: The pilot is responsible for certifying all medical equipment before flight
options.B: Medical equipment used in aircraft must be approved by the relevant Civil Aviation Authority (CAA/IAA/EASA) as airworthy — this covers electromagnetic compatibility with aircraft systems,structural integrity under crash forces, and compliance with weight-and-balance calculations; CE marking alone is insufficient
options.C: Aviation certification is only required for equipment permanently installed in the aircraft, not for portable kit bags carried by the medical team
options.D: CE marking on medical equipment automatically confers aviation certification
options.E: Any hospital-grade equipment can be used in aircraft without modification — medical standards supersede aviation standards

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.1.4 specifically covers aviation certification of medical equipment. Key principles: (1) Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) — medical equipment must not emit radio frequency interference that could affect aircraft avionics or communications; all equipment used in aircraft must be tested and approved for this; (2) Structural integrity — equipment must be able to withstand the g-forces experienced in a crash without fragmenting into projectiles (DO-160 standard for avionics environments in the USA; equivalent European standards); (3) Flammability — materials must meet aviation fire resistance standards; (4) Weight and balance — every item added to an aircraft affects its centre of gravity and maximum payload; weight must be declared and approved; (5) Securing — equipment must be mounted on aircraft-approved stretcher bridges and securing systems. CE marking indicates compliance with European health and safety standards for medical devices but does NOT confer aviation certification. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine confirms: equipment in vehicles should be "flight-tested to ensure compatibility with the aircraft's avionics systems, secured and selected on the basis of ease of use, compatibility, robustness, size and weight."

text: A retrieval physician completes a clinical shift and returns the equipment kit to base. Which of the following most accurately reflects their equipment governance responsibilities at this point?

options.A: Discard all single-use items; the biomedical team will restock for the next shift
options.B: Complete a systematic post-mission equipment check: document and report any faults or near-misses involving equipment, restock all
options.C: Post-mission equipment checks are only required after adverse events
options.D: The biomedical engineer will check all equipment — the clinical crew have no post-mission responsibilities
options.E: Lock the kit bag and hand it to the next crew without further action

explanation: Equipment governance applies at every phase — including post-mission. The Oxford Specialist Handbook and FICM/ICS transfer guidance both require systematic post-mission equipment management. Key responsibilities: (1) Fault reporting — any equipment malfunction, near-miss, or unexpected performance issue must be reported through the service's incident reporting system and, for significant issues, to the Medical and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) in the UK, or its Irish equivalent (HPRA), under the Medical Device Vigilance system; (2) Restock — all consumables used (drugs, airways, cannulae, IV fluids, oxygen) must be replaced before the kit is available for the next mission; (3) Battery charging — all battery-operated devices must be connected to charge between missions; (4) Cleaning and decontamination — equipment used in direct patient care must be decontaminated according to infection control guidelines; (5) Biomedical engineering — any equipment requiring calibration, repair, or software update must be identified and referred. Option E creates a patient safety risk — the next crew may deploy with incomplete or faulty equipment. Option D incorrectly removes clinical crew responsibility. The clinical team who used the equipment are best placed to identify what was used, what malfunctioned, and what requires restocking.

text: A retrieval team is preparing to enter a smoke-filled building to manage a casualty with suspected cyanide toxicity from a structure fire. Standard flight suits are worn. Which PPE assessment is most appropriate?

options.A: Administer hydroxocobalamin prophylactically to the team before entry
options.B: Flight suits provide adequate protection from cyanide — proceed with standard team PPE
options.C: A dynamic risk assessment identifies this as a HAZMAT scenario beyond the training and PPE of the retrieval team
options.D: Only the most senior team member needs enhanced PPE — others can use standard PPE
options.E: Apply an FFP3 respirator over the flight suit and proceed

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.2.3 requires knowledge of when PPE should be used, with the critical recognition that some scenarios exceed the available PPE capability. Cyanide toxicity from a structure fire presents specific challenges: hydrogen cyanide gas is produced from burning synthetic materials; it is immediately dangerous to life at concentrations >100 ppm; standard flight suits and FFP3 respirators provide NO protection against cyanide. The correct response applies the HAZMAT scene management principles: (1) Standard PPE is inadequate — identify the limitation; (2) Stage at the safe zone (cold zone, upwind, uphill where possible); (3) Request fire service HAZMAT team for hot zone entry and casualty decontamination; (4) Manage decontaminated casualties in the cold zone; (5) For casualties who escape the hot zone — initiate decontamination protocol (remove and bag clothing, water rinse) before clinical contact. Administering hydroxocobalamin (option A) to the team is not appropriate prophylaxis and does not replace adequate PPE. Option D creates unequal risk without benefit. Option E provides no protection against chemical toxicity. The Oxford Specialist Handbook and UK HAZMAT guidance are explicit: personal safety is paramount.

text: A retrieval team member is required to perform IV cannulation while wearing full HCID PPE (double gloves, coverall, PAPR). Which statement most accurately describes operating whilst using this PPE?

options.A: Clinical procedures can be performed in HCID PPE but require prior training and practice; fine motor skills are significantly impaired by double gloves
options.B: All clinical procedures become impossible in full HCID PPE — the patient should be palliated rather than treated
options.C: Clinical procedures cannot be performed while wearing HCID PPE — intubate the patient before donning PPE
options.D: Double gloves should be removed for all clinical procedures — infection control is secondary to procedural success
options.E: PAPR devices prevent bending over the patient — all procedures must be performed with the patient standing upright

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.2.6 requires candidates to "demonstrate the ability to operate whilst using PPE." The Oxford Specialist Handbook emphasises that PPE training must include practical clinical skills in full PPE. Key impairments: (1) Double gloves — significantly reduce tactile sensitivity and fine motor control; peripheral cannulation success rates fall substantially; (2) PAPR/respiratory protection — limits movement of the head and neck, affects posture during procedures; (3) Coverall — restricts joint movement, increases heat and fatigue; (4) Fogged visors — can impair vision during fine work. Mitigation strategies: (1) Intraosseous access (IO) — reliable in full PPE as it requires less tactile precision; (2) Ultrasound guidance — compensates for reduced tactile feedback for vascular access; (3) Larger cannula sizes — easier to handle with gloved hands; (4) Pre-mission preparation — pre-draw drugs, prepare equipment before donning full PPE; (5) Partner-assisted procedures — one operator holds structure, other inserts cannula. Regular simulation training in full PPE is essential — the first time a clinician operates in HCID PPE should not be at a live patient. Option D is dangerous — breaking PPE during patient contact with a Category A HCID pathogen risks fatal exposure.

text: A retrieval team carries a Dräger Oxylog 3000+ transport ventilator. A colleague at the receiving hospital asks whether the ventilator's tidal volume delivery is accurate at altitude. What is the most accurate response?

options.A: Altitude only affects ventilator performance above 10,000m — clinical altitudes are irrelevant
options.B: Gas-powered ventilators can over-deliver tidal volume at altitude because as atmospheric pressure falls, the fixed-pressure gas source delivers a larger volume of gas per breath than at sea level
options.C: Tidal volume decreases at altitude because thinner air contains fewer oxygen molecules
options.D: All transport ventilators deliver precisely set tidal volumes regardless of altitude — no adjustment is needed
options.E: Replace the transport ventilator with a bag-valve-mask above 2000m

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.3.3 requires understanding of how the retrieval environment may compromise medical device function. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine explicitly states: "Ventilator performance at altitude varies; a recent study at altitude up to 3000m showed: Oxylog 1000 — TV increased by 68% and RR decreased by 28%; Oxylog 2000 — TV increased by 29%; Oxylog 3000 — No change. So closely monitor TV and resp rate to ensure the vent is delivering what it's meant to with altitude changes." The mechanism: pneumatically driven (gas-powered) ventilators use the pressure differential between the oxygen source and atmospheric pressure to drive gas into the patient. As atmospheric pressure falls at altitude, a fixed-pressure gas source delivers a greater volume of gas per breath cycle — effectively increasing tidal volume above the set value. Microprocessor-controlled ventilators (like the Oxylog 3000+) measure actual exhaled volume and compensate, but this compensation is not perfect. Clinical implications: always verify exhaled tidal volume against set values at altitude; adjust settings if discrepancy identified; prefer ventilators with active closed-loop control for aeromedical transport. Option D is incorrect and potentially dangerous.

text: During retrieval, a ventilated patient's transport ventilator suddenly alarms "Low Pressure / Apnoea." The patient's SpO₂ is falling. The retrieval physician disconnects from the ventilator and begins hand ventilation — the bag inflates easily with good chest rise. What is the most likely cause of the alarm?

options.A: Bilateral tension pneumothorax
options.B: Circuit disconnection or leak (ventilator-circuit-ETT disconnect),
options.C: Complete airway obstruction from mucus plug
options.D: Ventilator software failure requiring factory reset
options.E: ETT cuff rupture causing complete loss of airway seal

explanation: This question tests systematic analysis of ventilator alarms (DipRTM subdomains 4.3.5 and 4.3.6). A "Low Pressure" alarm means the ventilator detects insufficient pressure during inspiration — most commonly caused by: (1) Circuit disconnection — the most common cause; gas escapes before reaching the patient; (2) Circuit leak — loose connection, cracked tubing, faulty valve; (3) ETT cuff leak — cuff deflated or ruptured, gas escapes around the tube; (4) Patient triggering — if the patient is breathing spontaneously at a rate faster than the set backup rate, many ventilators alarm if they don't detect a breath within the set apnoea interval. The key diagnostic finding here is: "bag inflates easily with good chest rise" when hand-ventilating. This immediately rules out: bilateral tension pneumothorax (would resist inflation), complete airway obstruction (would resist inflation), and complete ETT obstruction. The problem is in the circuit/ventilator-patient interface, NOT the airway or lung. Next step: reconnect from ventilator side, check all connections systematically (ventilator outlet → circuit → ETT connector → cuff pressure), identify and fix the disconnection point, then resume mechanical ventilation. Option A is incorrect because easy hand ventilation with good chest rise excludes tension pneumothorax.

text: A retrieval physician needs to establish definitive airway access for a patient with severe facial burns. Which airway device, available in the retrieval kit, is absolutely contraindicated as a primary airway in this scenario?

options.A: Cuffed endotracheal tube via video laryngoscopy
options.B: Surgical cricothyroidotomy as a last resort
options.C: Supraglottic airway (e.g. i-gel) as a temporary rescue device
options.D: Nasotracheal intubation via a nasal ETT as the primary airway management technique
options.E: Nasopharyngeal airway as a pre-oxygenation adjunct

explanation: DipRTM subdomains 4.3.1(a) and 1.12.7 address airway device selection in burns patients. Nasotracheal intubation is absolutely contraindicated in significant facial burns for several reasons: (1) Nasal passage oedema — burns affecting the face cause progressive oedema of nasal mucosa, making nasotracheal passage impossible or traumatic; (2) Singed nasal hairs indicate supraglottic involvement — nasal passage may be directly injured; (3) Epistaxis risk — passing a tube through inflamed, oedematous nasal mucosa causes haemorrhage that obscures the view and worsens the airway; (4) ETT securing — nasal ETTs secured with tape or ties will compress and exacerbate already compromised facial tissue. The correct approach in severe facial burns (DAS guidelines and BBA recommendations): RSI with video laryngoscopy (preferred — direct laryngoscopy is more difficult in facial oedema/distortion); a slightly smaller ETT size than normal due to subglottic oedema; surgical airway must be immediately available; decision to secure airway must be made early before oedema makes the airway unmanageable. Nasopharyngeal airways (option E) are relatively contraindicated in facial burns but may be briefly used as a pre-oxygenation adjunct if essential. Supraglottic airways (option C) may serve as a temporary rescue device in a cannot-intubate scenario.

text: A retrieval team is managing a patient with haemorrhagic shock following a ruptured AA

options.A: Use a mechanical rapid infuser (e.g. Level 1 or Belmont) capable of warming and delivering blood products at rates up to 500–1000 ml/min
options.B: Switch from the 14G peripheral cannulae to a triple-lumen central venous catheter — CVCs provide higher flow rates
options.C: Apply a pressure bag inflated to 300 mmHg around the blood product bag, attached to the existing 14G cannulae
options.D: They have two large-bore peripheral cannulae (14G) but need to rapidly infuse blood products. What is the fastest safe method of increasing infusion rate through existing peripheral IV access? A. Use a standard gravity drip with the bag elevated 2 metres above the patient
options.E: Dilute the blood product with normal saline to reduce viscosity

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.3.1(f) covers devices for administering medicines and blood products. Flow rate through an IV cannula is governed by Hagen-Poiseuille's law: flow is proportional to the fourth power of the radius (gauge) and the pressure gradient, and inversely proportional to viscosity and length. Key principle: a short, wide-bore (14G) peripheral cannula flows faster than a long, narrow central venous catheter lumen. For rapid blood product administration: (1) Pressure bags at 300 mmHg — the most practical retrieval solution; can increase flow rates 3–5 fold compared with gravity; (2) The Belmont or Level 1 rapid infuser (option A) can deliver 500–1000 ml/min AND warms blood products to prevent hypothermia — the gold standard for massive transfusion scenarios — but is bulky and not always carried by retrieval teams; (3) IO access — if IV access is inadequate, IO allows rapid infusion at near-IV rates. Option B is incorrect: CVCs are longer and narrower than 14G peripheral cannulae — they have LOWER flow rates. Standard gravity (option D) is insufficient. Dilution of blood products (option E) reduces viscosity slightly but also reduces oxygen-carrying capacity — not a recommended strategy. For MICAS/NAS CCRS practice, pressure bags are standard kit items.

text: Regarding tourniquet application for life-threatening limb haemorrhage during retrieval, which of the following statements is most accurate?

options.A: Tourniquets are never appropriate in civilian retrieval medicine — direct pressure is always sufficient
options.B: Tourniquets should be removed every 30 minutes to allow limb reperfusion
options.C: A commercial windlass tourniquet (e.g. Combat Application Tourniquet
options.D: Improvised tourniquets (e.g. belts, ropes) are equally effective as commercial windlass devices
options.E: A tourniquet should be applied as distally as possible on the limb to minimise tissue ischaemia

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.3.1(c) covers haemorrhage control devices, and this topic is addressed in the ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (Chapter 15). Current UK military and civilian evidence (Tactical Combat Casualty Care, JRCALC guidelines, NICE NG39) establishes: (1) Indication — life-threatening haemorrhage from a limb that cannot be controlled with direct pressure; junctional zone haemorrhage (groin, axilla) may require junctional tourniquet (JETT/iTClamp); (2) Placement — 5–8 cm (2–3 inches) proximal to the wound to occlude blood flow completely; too distal = ineffective; (3) Tightening — must occlude arterial flow completely (no distal pulse); partial occlusion is worse than no tourniquet — increases venous congestion without stopping arterial inflow; (4) Time recording — essential for informing surgical teams of ischaemia duration; limbs tolerate ischaemia for 2–6 hours; (5) No removal in the field — removing a tourniquet that has achieved haemostasis risks re-bleeding and coagulopathy cascade; removal should only occur in a surgical facility. Commercial windlass tourniquets (CAT, SOFTT-W) are significantly more effective than improvised devices — improvised tourniquets frequently fail to occlude arterial flow (option D is incorrect).

text: A retrieval team arrives to find a patient with suspected acute pericardial tamponade in extremis. Echocardiography confirms a large pericardial effusion with right ventricular collapse. The transport stretcher is loaded and the team is about to depart. What equipment must be immediately available, and what is the correct approach?

options.A: The team should have pericardiocentesis equipment available (20 ml syringe, long 18G needle, 3-way tap, connecting tubing) and perform
options.B: Pericardiocentesis requires general anaesthesia — not possible in the retrieval setting
options.C: Administer adrenaline 1mg IV push and transfer immediately
options.D: Transfer immediately — pericardiocentesis is a hospital procedure and cannot be performed by a retrieval team
options.E: Increase the IV fluid rate to maintain preload and transfer rapidly — fluids compensate for tamponade during transfer

explanation: DipRTM subdomains 4.3.1(d/e) and 1.5 cover circulatory support devices and management of emergencies during retrieval. Acute pericardial tamponade is one of the reversible causes of PEA arrest (part of the 4Hs and 4Ts). A patient with haemodynamic compromise from tamponade will not survive transport without drainage — the stress of movement, position changes, and reduced cardiac reserve in transit will precipitate arrest. The Oxford Specialist Handbook (Chapter 6: Ultrasound — Ultrasound-guided techniques) specifically lists "pericardial aspiration" as a procedure to stabilise patients prior to transfer. Technique: (1) Confirm with ultrasound; (2) Position: needle at 45° below xiphisternum aimed towards left shoulder; (3) Insert under continuous aspiration — a rush of fluid (blood in haemopericardium, serous fluid in effusion) indicates entry to pericardium; (4) 3-way tap allows intermittent drainage without removing needle; (5) Even 20–30 ml drainage can produce dramatic haemodynamic improvement. Retrieval equipment should include pericardiocentesis kit (long needle, 50ml syringe, 3-way tap). Fluid loading (option E) may temporise briefly but does not address the mechanical obstruction. General anaesthesia (option B) is not required — the procedure is performed under local anaesthesia (lidocaine skin infiltration) or without in extremis.

text: A retrieval physician is about to perform an emergency needle decompression for suspected tension pneumothorax in a large (120 kg) man. They have a standard 14G cannula available. What is the most important practical consideration specific to this patient?

options.A: Standard needle length (4.5 cm including hub) is adequate for all adult patients
options.B: Obese patients should never undergo needle decompression — proceed directly to formal chest drain insertion
options.C: Needle decompression should always be performed in the 5th intercostal space mid-axillary line regardless of body habitus
options.D: Standard 14G cannulae may be insufficient length to penetrate the chest wall reliably in obese patients
options.E: Two standard 14G cannulae inserted side-by-side will adequately replace a longer needle

explanation: This question addresses a clinically important limitation of standard needle decompression in retrieval. DipRTM subdomain 4.3.1(b) covers ventilatory support devices, and subdomain 1.5 addresses management of emergencies during retrieval. Evidence basis: Multiple studies (Harcke et al., Aho et al.) demonstrate that the standard 14G × 4.5 cm cannula fails to reach the pleural space in a significant proportion of patients — up to 30–50% in obese individuals where chest wall thickness exceeds 4.5 cm at the second intercostal space mid-clavicular line. Current JRCALC guidelines and ATLS (10th edition) acknowledge this limitation and recommend: (1) In suspected tension pneumothorax — consider immediate finger thoracostomy (bilateral in the peri-arrest patient) rather than needle decompression; (2) If needle decompression is performed, use the longest available needle (some services carry 8–10 cm needles for this purpose); (3) The 4th/5th ICS mid-axillary line (alternative site) may have less chest wall thickness than the 2nd ICS MCL and is preferred by some services. Finger thoracostomy (blunt dissection into the pleural space with a finger) is more reliable, especially in obese patients, and is now taught in ATLS and JRCALC. Option A is incorrect — failure rate with standard needles is clinically significant.

text: Which of the following statements about point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) in the retrieval environment is most accurate?

options.A: Ultrasound-guided procedures (central venous access, pericardiocentesis, nerve blocks) cannot be performed in the field due to sterility requirements
options.B: POCUS requires specialist training that retrieval physicians cannot acquire during a 12-month fellowship
options.C: The E-FAST examination (6-quadrant scan for free fluid, tamponade, pneumothorax, haemothorax) should be completed before any retrieval of a trauma patient and can be performed in 1–2 minutes
options.D: Ultrasound causes electromagnetic interference with aircraft avionics and must not be used in flight
options.E: Ultrasound is only useful for identifying pneumothorax — other indications are not relevant to retrieval

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.3.1(l) covers imaging and diagnostic devices in retrieval. The Oxford Specialist Handbook devotes a section to ultrasound (Chapter 6), and the ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (Chapter 14: Near Patient Testing) confirms: "A complete E-FAST can be performed in 1–2 minutes by experienced clinicians and can be performed prior to travel or en route to the receiving facility, thus minimising delays at the scene." The E-FAST examination: Morrison's pouch (right upper quadrant — hepatorenal free fluid), splenorenal space (left upper quadrant), pouch of Douglas (pelvis), pericardial sac (subcostal/parasternal), and bilateral anterior chest (pneumothorax/haemothorax). Additional POCUS applications in retrieval (all referenced in the textbooks): massive PE (dilated RV with paradoxical septal motion), tamponade (pericardial effusion with RV collapse), hypovolaemia (underfilled ventricles, collapsing IVC), aortic dissection (aortic flap), and AAA. Regarding aviation compatibility (option D): "Experience to date suggests that ultrasound does not cause any electromagnetic interference with aircraft systems, and has been used in Scandinavian countries at low ambient temperatures" (ABC). Option A is incorrect — ultrasound-guided procedures with appropriate aseptic technique are standard retrieval practice. POCUS training (option B) is achievable within a 12-month fellowship and is a core MICAS/NAS CCRS competency.

text: A vacuum mattress is used during a retrieval of a patient with suspected spinal injury. Which of the following best describes the purpose and correct use of a vacuum mattress?

options.A: Vacuum mattresses actively heat the patient — they are primarily used for hypothermia management
options.B: The vacuum mattress is inflated with air to create a cushioning effect around the patient, then sealed to maintain inflation
options.C: Vacuum mattresses are contraindicated in spinal injuries — only long spinal boards should be used
options.D: The vacuum mattress is filled with polystyrene beads
options.E: The vacuum mattress should remain sealed at maximum vacuum throughout transfer with no further adjustment required

explanation: DipRTM subdomains 4.3.1(h) and 1.10 cover immobilisation devices and spinal injury management. The vacuum mattress (e.g. Skedco, Medi-Vac) is a key piece of retrieval immobilisation equipment: (1) Construction — polystyrene beads within an airtight outer shell; (2) Mechanism — when air is evacuated via a one-way valve, the beads compact and the mattress rigidly conforms to the patient's body contours, providing circumferential immobilisation superior to a flat spinal board; (3) Advantages over spinal board — more comfortable (reduces pressure areas in long transfers), better immobilisation of irregular body surfaces (e.g. obese patients, patients with kyphosis), radiolucent (X-rays can be taken through it); (4) Altitude consideration — critically important for the DipRTM — at altitude, the air within the mattress expands (Boyle's Law), causing the mattress to soften and lose rigidity; this must be anticipated and the mattress re-evacuated during ascent to maintain immobilisation. Similarly, on descent, the mattress may become over-rigid and uncomfortable. Option E is therefore incorrect. Option A is wrong — vacuum mattresses have no heating function and can actually cause heat loss (conduct cold). Current spinal immobilisation guidelines (JRCALC, RCSLT) favour vacuum mattresses over long boards for prolonged transfers.

text: An invasive arterial line is in situ in a patient being transferred by road ambulance. During the transfer, the arterial trace on the monitor shows a dampened waveform. What is the most likely cause and appropriate response?

options.A: The arterial trace becomes dampened in all patients during road transport — switch to NIBP measurement
options.B: The transducer is positioned incorrectly (too high or too low relative to the patient's heart); re-zero and reposition at the level of the right atrium (4th ICS, mid-axillary line)
options.C: A dampened waveform indicates cardiac arrest — begin CPR immediately
options.D: Dampened waveform always indicates a clot in the cannula — remove and replace immediately
options.E: The arterial line is correctly functioning — dampened traces are normal during road transport due to vibration artefact; no action required

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.3.1(k) covers invasive monitoring devices. The Oxford Specialist Handbook states: "For accurate monitoring the arterial line transducer should be fixed to the patient at the level of the right atrium." In road transport, transducer positioning relative to the patient frequently changes (patient tilts, stretcher angles change during acceleration/braking, road camber). Causes of dampened arterial waveform (systematic approach): (1) Transducer malpositioning — most common during transport; re-zero and reposition; (2) Air bubble in the transducer or tubing — causes damping; flush with 0.9% NaCl; (3) Partial clot or fibrin at cannula tip — aspirate and flush; (4) Kinking of the arterial line; (5) Vasospasm around the cannula. The mathematical relationship: for every 10 cm of height discrepancy between transducer and heart, blood pressure reading changes by 7.5 mmHg (0.1 mmHg/mmH₂O × 75 = 7.5 mmHg/10cm). Transducer positioned too high → falsely low reading; too low → falsely high reading. Road transport consideration: secure the transducer to the patient's chest wall (not to the stretcher rail) to maintain consistent relative positioning throughout the journey. Option E is incorrect — dampened trace reduces the accuracy of blood pressure assessment and must be addressed.

text: A retrieval team in a remote area loses their primary radio communication system mid-mission. Which of the following correctly describes the communication contingency approach?

options.A: Continue the mission in radio silence and report on return to base
options.B: Abort the mission immediately — retrieval is not safe without primary radio communications
options.C: Transfer communications responsibility to the patient's family
options.D: Request the pilot to send Morse code signals from the aircraft
options.E: A layered communication redundancy approach should be used: switch to secondary radio system (e.g. UHF backup), then satellite phone, then personal mobile phone — each layer provides a backup if the previous fails

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.4.1(a) covers communication equipment in retrieval, and subdomain 3.8 addresses contingency planning. The Oxford Specialist Handbook and ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine both emphasise the importance of communication redundancy in retrieval operations. Retrieval services should carry multiple communication systems: (1) Primary — TETRA digital radio (UK) or equivalent service radio system; (2) Secondary — UHF/VHF radio backup; (3) Tertiary — satellite phone (essential for remote/offshore retrievals); (4) Quaternary — personal mobile phones (limited coverage but widely available). TETRA (Terrestrial Trunked Radio) is used by UK emergency services — it is digital, secure, provides interoperability between services, and has broad coverage. Satellite phones are the primary backup for areas without cellular coverage. The ABC of Transfer states: "Personal mobile phones add an extra level of resilience when planning and during transfers if there is a problem with the primary communication source." The mission should continue (option B is wrong) — communication failure alone does not make the mission unsafe if the team are already committed; however, the coordination centre must be notified by any available means. Maintaining mission oversight is the purpose — not blind radio silence (option A).

text: A retrieval team is navigating to a remote rural location for a primary retrieval. Their GPS unit fails. What is the most appropriate backup navigation strategy?

options.A: Request the patient's family to guide the crew to the scene by phone
options.B: Use the aircraft pilot's onboard navigation systems (which are independent of the medical team's GPS) as primary navigation
options.C: Navigate by estimating direction from the position of the sun
options.D: Abandon the mission — GPS is mandatory for all retrievals
options.E: Switch to satellite navigation on a personal mobile phone — this is equivalent to a dedicated GPS unit

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.4.1(c) covers navigation equipment. The key principles: (1) The aircraft pilot has onboard navigation systems (GPS, radio navigation, or INS) that are entirely separate from the medical team's equipment — for air missions, navigation failure of the medical GPS does not affect the aircraft's ability to reach the destination; (2) For road missions, backup navigation relies on: paper maps and grid references (the coordination centre should provide precise grid references in OS six-figure format), local knowledge, landmark navigation, and mobile phone navigation apps as a secondary option; (3) Grid references — standardised communication using OS map grid references prevents ambiguity about location, particularly in rural areas where addresses are absent. DipRTM subdomain 3.8 discusses the coordination centre's role in providing logistical support including location information during the mission. Mobile phones (option E) may be used as a backup but have significant limitations: battery life, signal coverage in remote areas, and accuracy may be insufficient. They are not equivalent to dedicated navigation units. Paper map reading is an explicit DipRTM competency: subdomain 4.4.1(c) includes "basic navigation equipment including map reading."

text: A retrieval team is preparing a noradrenaline infusion for a patient with septic shock. The standard concentration used by the service is 4mg in 50ml (80 mcg/ml). A patient weighing 75kg requires 0.2 mcg/kg/min. What is the correct infusion rate in ml/hr?

options.A: 9 ml/hr
options.B: 18 ml/hr
options.C: 0.9 ml/hr
options.D: 4.5 ml/hr
options.E: 11.25 ml/hr

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.10 requires safe prescribing and dispensing of medicines, including accurate drug calculations. Step-by-step calculation: Dose required: 0.2 mcg/kg/min × 75 kg = 15 mcg/min. Convert to per hour: 15 mcg/min × 60 min = 900 mcg/hr. Concentration: 4mg in 50ml = 4000 mcg in 50ml = 80 mcg/ml. Rate: 900 mcg/hr ÷ 80 mcg/ml = 11.25 ml/hr. Answer: C — 11.25 ml/hr. Verification: 11.25 ml/hr × 80 mcg/ml = 900 mcg/hr ÷ 60 min = 15 mcg/min ÷ 75 kg = 0.2 mcg/kg/min ✓. Safe drug calculation is a fundamental patient safety competency in retrieval medicine. The retrieval environment — with noise, vibration, fatigue, and time pressure — creates conditions where calculation errors are more likely. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine recommends: double-checking all calculations; using a standardised drug concentration for each vasoactive drug across the service (eliminates concentration confusion); considering pre-printed calculation reference cards. Weight-based calculations should always use confirmed weight (or reliable estimate) and should be double-checked by a second team member where possible. Getting this wrong by a factor of 2 (option A or B) could produce life-threatening under- or over-dosing.

text: The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine describes principles of good medicines management in retrieval. Which of the following best describes the correct approach to drug storage and controlled drugs during retrieval?

options.A: Drugs should be stored in a purpose-designed carry case with padding or elastic loops
options.B: All drugs can be stored at room temperature without any special considerations
options.C: Pre-filled syringes should never be used in retrieval — all drugs must be drawn up fresh at the patient's bedside
options.D: Drug expiry dates in retrieval kit bags can be ignored — the environmental conditions preserve the drugs
options.E: Controlled drugs regulations do not apply in the prehospital or retrieval environment — only hospital pharmacies are regulated

explanation: DipRTM subdomains 4.5.1–4.5.5 cover medicines management and controlled drugs legislation. Key principles from the ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (Chapter 19): (1) Storage — purpose-designed carry cases with padding or elastic loops prevent breakage of glass ampoules; plastic ampoules preferred where available; (2) Controlled drugs — the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 and associated regulations apply to retrieval services as they do to hospitals; retrieval services are legally required to: maintain a controlled drugs register; store CDs in a locked container; account for all CD stock with dual-witness signatures for administration; report discrepancies immediately; (3) Temperature — certain drugs require refrigeration: some muscle relaxants (suxamethonium in some formulations), blood products, some antibiotics, and insulin; retrieval services must plan for temperature-controlled transport for these items; (4) Expiry dates — must be regularly checked and rotated; (5) Pre-filled syringes — the ABC specifically recommends "the use of prefilled syringes for time critical medications (e.g. cardiac arrest medications) may be warranted as well as the drawing up of emergency drugs (e.g. for RSI) at the beginning of a shift/tasking" — option C is therefore incorrect. Option E is incorrect — CD regulations apply to all healthcare settings including retrieval.

text: A retrieval team is transporting a patient on a ketamine infusion (maintaining dissociative sedation for a behavioural disturbance case) via helicopter. Ketamine is a Schedule 2 Controlled Drug in Ireland and the UK. During the flight, an additional bolus dose is required. Which of the following most accurately reflects the correct legal and practical approach?

options.A: Ketamine is not a controlled drug — no special documentation is required
options.B: The administration must be documented in the patient care record with dose, time, and route
options.C: Controlled drug documentation can be completed retrospectively at the end of the shift without any in-flight record-keeping
options.D: Administration of additional controlled drug doses in flight requires contacting the coordination centre for authorisation each time
options.E: Controlled drug regulations are suspended during aeromedical operations — administer freely as clinically required

explanation: DipRTM subdomain 4.5.13 requires demonstration of "compliance with legislation related to controlled drugs." Ketamine is indeed a Schedule 2 Controlled Drug in Ireland (Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 and associated regulations) and the UK (Misuse of Drugs Act 1971, rescheduled from Schedule 4 to Schedule 2 in 2014). Legal requirements: (1) Administration must be documented contemporaneously in the patient clinical record with: drug name, dose, route, time, batch number, administering clinician signature; (2) Where regulations require a witness (varies by jurisdiction and setting — typically hospital requirement, may differ for retrieval), the second crew member fulfils this role; (3) Remaining controlled drug stock must be returned to base with documented quantities; (4) The controlled drugs register must be updated on return, recording all doses administered and quantities remaining; (5) Any discrepancy between administered quantity and remaining stock must be immediately reported. Option E is dangerous and legally incorrect. Option A is incorrect — ketamine is Schedule 2 since 2014 in the UK and Schedule 2 in Ireland. Option D would create unsafe delays in time-critical sedation management. Option C (retrospective documentation) is legally inadequate and creates a risk of inaccurate recording.

text: A retrieval team is preparing to transfer a patient who requires an oxygen flow rate of 15 L/min via a non-rebreather mask during a 90-minute road transfer. They have access to two F-size oxygen cylinders (each containing approximately 1360 L at full). How much oxygen will be consumed and is the supply adequate?

options.A: Oxygen consumption cannot be calculated for non-ventilated patients
options.B: 2700 L consumed — both cylinders are insufficient
options.C: 900 L consumed — one F-cylinder is adequate with reserve
options.D: 810 L consumed — one F-cylinder is more than adequate
options.E: 1350 L consumed

explanation: DipRTM subdomains 4.5.6 and 1.4.4 cover medical gas management. Calculation: Flow rate: 15 L/min. Duration: 90 minutes. Consumption: 15 × 90 = 1350 L. One F-cylinder contains approximately 1360 L. One cylinder provides: 1360 L ÷ 15 L/min = 90.7 minutes — marginally adequate with virtually no reserve. A safety reserve of at least 30–50% is recommended for any transfer. Therefore: carry both F-cylinders (2720 L total), providing 2720 ÷ 15 = 181 minutes of supply — adequate with reserve for a 90-minute transfer. Additional considerations: (1) Transfer may take longer than planned — traffic, clinical delays on arrival; (2) If the patient deteriorates and requires higher-flow oxygen or intubation with ventilator (which uses oxygen as driving gas), consumption increases dramatically; (3) The ABC of Transfer states: "ensure the supply is adequate prior to departure. Calculate how much is likely to be required, and add a generous amount for delays. This particularly applies to patients on high-flow or non-invasive ventilation who use a lot of oxygen." Running out of oxygen during transfer is a preventable, potentially fatal complication. Pre-mission oxygen calculation is an explicitly examined DipRTM competency.

text: Aviation regulations impose specific restrictions on the carriage and use of medical gases in aircraft. Which of the following most accurately describes these restrictions?

options.A: Medical oxygen cylinders are banned from all aircraft — passengers requiring oxygen must use supplemental oxygen provided by the airline
options.B: Any oxygen cylinder can be used in any aircraft as long as the patient requires supplemental oxygen
options.C: Aviation oxygen cylinders (used by pilots) and medical oxygen are identical and interchangeable
